grammar.usage: Express a general truth or definition
grammar.example: If you have faith in something, you believe in something you cannot prove.

grammar.usage: Express a condition possible in the present or future
grammar.example: If you have a birthday party, you'll get loads of cool presents.

grammar.usage: Express an imaginary or impossible condition in the present or future, or give advice
grammar.example: If we were older, we could go on holiday on our own.

grammar.usage: Express an imaginary or impossible condition in the past and its result in the past
grammar.example: If it hadn't been for your help, I wouldn't have succeeded.

grammar.usage: Express an imaginary or impossible condition in the past and its result in the present, or vice-versa
grammar.example: If I had listened to my parents, I wouldn't be in so much trouble now.

rows:
Used to express a general truth or definition
EX: If you have faith in something, you believe in something you cannot prove.
If + a present tense, WILL + Vbare

Used to express a condition possible in the present or future
EX: If you have a birthday party, you'll get loads of cool presents.
EX: If you're working till 7p.m., we'll have dinner at about 8 p.m.
EX: If you have revised properly, you won't have any problems in the test next week.

If + S1 + Vs/es, (S2) + WILL + Vbare
→ Should + S1 + Vbare, WILL + Vbare

EX: If he should ring, I will tell him the news.
→ Should he ring, I will tell him the news.

EX: If he has free time, he'll play tennis.
→ Should he have free time, he'll play tennis.

bullets:
Depending on the meaning, 'will' can be replaced by: may, might, could, shall, should, ought to, have to
EX: If you have a birthday party, you might get loads of cool presents.

Can use 'should + Vbare' instead of present simple to express a condition that is possible but has a lower probability.
EX: If you should bump into Alex at the concert, you'll be able to get a lift home.

To give instructions or commands about a possible situation, we can use the structure: If + a present tense, [imperative]
EX: If you decide to have a birthday party, tell me!

rows: If + past simple / past continuous, WOULD + Vbare

Used to express an imaginary or impossible condition in the present or future, or to give advice.
EX: If we were older, we could go on holiday on our own.
EX: If I could drive, I'd buy a new car.

If + S1 + V2/ed / (+ was/were Ving), (S2) + WOULD + Vbare
→ Were + S1 + TO + Vbare (/ TO BE + Ving), WOULD + Vbare

EX: If I were a bird, I would fly.
→ Were I a bird, I would fly.

EX: If I learnt Russian, I would read a Russian book.
→ Were I to learn Russian, I would read a Russian book.
If + past perfect (simple or continuous), WOULD + have + V3/ed

Used to express an imaginary or impossible condition in the past and its result in the past.
EX: If it hadn't been for your help, I wouldn't have succeeded.

If + S1 + had + V3/ed / (+ been Ving), (S2) WOULD + have + V3/ed
→ HAD + S1 + V3/ed / (+ been Ving), (S2) would + have + V3/ed

EX: If it hadn't been for your help, I wouldn't have succeeded.
→ Had it not been for your help, I wouldn't have succeeded.

EX: If the situation had worsened, they would have been prepared to send in a peacekeeping force.
→ Had the situation worsened, they would have been prepared to send in a peacekeeping force.

bullets: Besides 'would', other modals can be used: might, could, should

rows: If + past perfect (simple / continuous), would + Vbare

Used to express an imaginary or impossible condition in the past and its result in the present.
EX: If I had listened to my parents, I wouldn't be in so much trouble now. (meaning: We are in trouble now because we didn't listen to our parents in the past)

If + S1 + had + V3/ed / (+ been Ving), (S2) + would + Vbare
→ Had + S1 + V3/ed / (+ been Ving), (S2) + would + Vbare

EX: If I hadn't told her about this, she wouldn't be sad now.
→ Had I not told her about this, she wouldn't be sad now.
If + past (simple / continuous), would + have + V3/ed

Used to express an imaginary or impossible condition in the present and its result in the past.
EX: If I had a mobile, I would have called you last night. (meaning: We didn't call last night because we don't have a mobile now)

If + S1 + past (simple / continuous), (S2) + would + have + V3/ed
→ Were + S1 + TO + Vbare / (+ to be Ving), (S2) + would + have + V3/ed

EX: If I had a mobile, I would have called you last night.
→ Were I to have a mobile, I would have called you last night.

bullets: Conditional clause before the result clause → comma is required
Conditional clause after the result clause → comma is not required

vocabulary.meaning: the head of an elected government
vocabulary.example: The prime minister announced the new economic policies yesterday.

vocabulary.meaning: a member of the government who is in charge of a department
vocabulary.example: The minister of education visited our school to discuss the new curriculum.

vocabulary.meaning: completely and with great attention to detail
vocabulary.example: The police thoroughly investigated the crime scene.

vocabulary.meaning: a result or effect of an action or condition, typically one that is unwelcome
vocabulary.example: You must accept the consequences of your decisions.

vocabulary.meaning: a small body of matter from outer space that enters the earth's atmosphere, appearing as a streak of light
vocabulary.example: We saw a bright meteor streak across the night sky.

vocabulary.meaning: side by side and having the same distance continuously between them
vocabulary.example: The road runs parallel to the railway line.

vocabulary.meaning: a game of chance in which tickets are sold and a drawing determines the prize winners
vocabulary.example: She won a million dollars in the national lottery.

vocabulary.meaning: an extra wheel with a tyre on it kept in a vehicle in case of a puncture
vocabulary.example: Always make sure you have a spare tyre in the boot of your car.

vocabulary.meaning: a group of soldiers sent to a conflict area to maintain peace
vocabulary.example: The United Nations sent a peacekeeping force to the region.
The weather began to worsen as the storm approached.
